topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sight hit me like a physical blow. Where the towering oak had stood, there was now only a jagged scar on the hillside, a raw wound in the landscape. I walked toward it slowly, my boots crunching on the disturbed earth, and a tight knot began to tighten in my chest. It was the same spot, the one I’d painstakingly nurtured as a small child, the one I’d sung lullabies to. Now, nothing but stumps and exposed roots. I stopped, running a hand over the rough bark of a nearby, much younger sapling, a gesture devoid of warmth. "Well," I murmured, the sound dry and brittle in the silence, "that's that, then." I turned away, the movement sharp, refusing to let my gaze linger on the wreckage.

[story 2]

I found myself standing at the perimeter of the clearing, the afternoon sun glinting off the freshly cut wood. My jaw was clenched so tight I could feel the strain in my molars. I remembered the sheer pride I’d felt when that sapling finally put on its first sturdy branch, the tangible proof of my effort. Now, it was just debris, carelessly tossed aside. A scoff escaped my lips, a short, sharp expulsion of air. "Amateur," I muttered under my breath, the word tasting like ash. I surveyed the empty space, cataloging the absence with a cool, assessing eye. There was no sorrow in the observation, only a deep-seated judgment about the carelessness that had erased something I had once valued.

[story 3]

The smell of freshly cut timber hung heavy in the air, overpowering the scent of damp soil I had associated with that spot for decades. I approached the clearing with deliberate, measured steps, my shoulders slightly hunched as if bracing against something unpleasant. When I finally saw the devastation—the sheer, brutal removal of the tree I’d planted as a boy—a flicker of something cold settled in my stomach. I didn't rush; I simply stood there, my arms crossed over my chest, my gaze sweeping over the ragged edges of the cut. A slow, almost imperceptible shake of my head followed. "So," I said, my voice low and flat, "that’s how it ends. Nothing lasts."
